title: Rivermark Compromise
content:
The Rivermark Compromise (1873) was a diplomatic agreement in which Ingrid Lannick, army liaison of Northern League of Kestrel Bay, helped finalize terms at Westmere.

Negotiations.

Ingrid Lannick, serving as army liaison, chaired preliminary talks that began in spring 1872. Neutral observers from neighboring cantons attended the sessions.

Reactions.

Newspapers in Northern League of Kestrel Bay praised the compromise, while opposition leaders argued that naval access points near Westmere were not sufficiently protected.

Implementation.

Commission reports from 1874 show gradual demobilization and the reopening of overland routes through the Westmere basin.

Historical assessment.

Modern historians view the Rivermark Compromise as a stabilizing milestone, crediting Ingrid Lannick's insistence on verifiable inspection clauses.

Background.

By the early 1873s, diplomats from Northern League of Kestrel Bay sought a settlement after years of border incidents near Westmere.
